title: Listening - Part 4: Training Search and Rescue Dogs
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a man called Leo, who trains dogs used in search and rescue operations. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My guest is Leo Grant, who trains dogs for search and rescue work. Leo, how did you get involved in this unusual job? || Leo: I had a pet dog as a child, but I never imagined training working dogs. A few years ago, heavy rain caused flooding in my region. [24]I volunteered at a rescue centre during the floods and saw trained dogs helping teams search damaged buildings[/24]. I was impressed not only by the dogs, but also by the careful relationship between each dog and handler. || Presenter: Are some breeds always better than others? || Leo: Certain breeds are common because they are active and learn quickly, but breed alone tells you very little. [25]The quality I look for first is a confident dog that is keen to search and remains interested in people[/25]. A clever dog that becomes frightened in unfamiliar places would struggle in this work. || Presenter: What do new handlers find most difficult? || Leo: Most handlers are caring and organised, so they learn practical routines. The challenging step is emotional. [26]They must trust the dog's signals even when they themselves cannot see or hear any sign of a missing person[/26]. A dog’s nose can identify information long before a human understands what is happening. || Presenter: Where do the dogs practise? || Leo: We train in woodland and empty buildings, but real searches are rarely quiet. There might be traffic, crowds or other animals nearby. [27]We deliberately practise in noisy public places so dogs learn to continue working despite distractions[/27]. Of course, we always keep them controlled and never disturb members of the public. || Presenter: Is there one rescue that stays in your memory? || Leo: There is. A child went missing near a campsite, and volunteers had searched paths for hours. My dog suddenly pulled towards an area of thick bushes. [28]He found the child hidden beneath branches, cold and frightened but fortunately unharmed[/28]. Seeing the family reunited reminded me why all the training matters. || Presenter: Work like that must be stressful for the dogs. || Leo: It can be tiring, but well-trained dogs enjoy searching. After an operation, we don’t immediately demand more exercises. [29]We give them rest and a favourite game, so work finishes with something positive and they can recover[/29]. Looking after their wellbeing is part of being professional. || Presenter: What should people do if they see a rescue dog working? || Leo: Most people want to be friendly, which is understandable. But [30]unless the handler invites you, don’t call to the dog or try to touch it, because its attention needs to stay on the search[/30]. The kindest thing is to give the team space. || Presenter: Leo, thank you. || Leo: Thank you very much.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What encouraged Leo to become involved in rescue dog training?

options.A: Helping at a centre during a flood emergency.
options.B: Training his childhood pet to find objects.
options.C: Meeting a rescue dog trainer at school.

questions.explanation: Leo volunteered during flooding and observed rescue dogs searching damaged buildings.

questions.question: What matters most to Leo when selecting a rescue dog?

options.A: It belongs to a breed traditionally used for searching.
options.B: It is confident and motivated to search.
options.C: It is physically larger than most other dogs.

questions.explanation: He first looks for confidence, interest in searching and comfort around people.

questions.question: What can be difficult for new handlers?

options.A: Interpreting the dog's signal without visible evidence.
options.B: Keeping the dog physically fit for long searches.
options.C: Remembering the routines used during training sessions.

questions.explanation: Handlers have to trust a dog's signal even before they can detect anyone themselves.

questions.question: Why are dogs trained in busy public locations?

options.A: So local people can learn about their work.
options.B: So handlers can test several dogs together.
options.C: So dogs can focus despite noise and movement.

questions.explanation: Leo uses noisy places to teach dogs to keep working when distractions are present.

questions.question: What happened in the rescue Leo remembers most clearly?

options.A: His dog found a missing child in thick vegetation.
options.B: A volunteer showed him a new way to search paths.
options.C: A family helped guide his dog back to a campsite.

questions.explanation: His dog located a missing child hidden beneath branches near a campsite.

questions.question: How does Leo look after dogs following an operation?

options.A: He lets them spend time with other dogs.
options.B: He gives them rest and an enjoyable activity.
options.C: He repeats an easy search to improve confidence.

questions.explanation: After operations, the dogs rest and play a favourite game to recover positively.

questions.question: What advice does Leo give to members of the public?

options.A: Report immediately if a dog seems tired.
options.B: Help the handler by pointing out possible locations.
options.C: Avoid distracting a dog that is searching.

questions.explanation: He asks people not to call or touch a working dog unless invited.
